question: Implement a `Book` class with `title` and `author` (both `std::string`). Provide a default constructor, a parameterized constructor, and a copy constructor. Demonstrate their usage by creating objects in different ways.

test_cases.input: Book b1; b1.display(); Book b2("The Hobbit", "J.R.R. Tolkien"); b2.display(); Book b3 = b2; b3.display();
test_cases.expected:
Default constructor called for Book: Untitled by Unknown
Book: "Untitled" by Unknown
Parameterized constructor called for Book: The Hobbit by J.R.R. Tolkien
Book: "The Hobbit" by J.R.R. Tolkien
Copy constructor called for Book: The Hobbit by Unknown Author
Book: "The Hobbit" by Unknown Author

test_cases.input: Book b4("1984", "George Orwell"); b4.display(); Book b5(b4); b5.display();
test_cases.expected:
Parameterized constructor called for Book: 1984 by George Orwell
Book: "1984" by George Orwell
Copy constructor called for Book: 1984 by Unknown Author
Book: "1984" by Unknown Author

driver: int main() { Book book1; book1.display(); Book book2("The Hobbit", "J.R.R. Tolkien"); book2.display(); Book book3 = book2; book3.display(); Book book4("1984", "George Orwell"); book4.display(); Book book5(book4); book5.display(); return 0; }
errors: The copy constructor `Book(const Book& other)` incorrectly initializes the `author` member to a fixed string "Unknown Author" instead of copying `other.author`. This leads to copied books having an incorrect author.
